header_info.term_info: Term 3 Scope & Sequence | March - June 2025-2026 | American Curriculum | ADEK Compliant | Prepared by: Mr. Ahmed Ali - Teacher of Moral, Social, and Cultural Studies (English Medium, Cycles 2–3)

lessons.learning_objective: SWBAT define civic engagement and active citizenship, and provide examples of how people contribute to their communities.
lessons.success_criteria:
I can define civic engagement and active citizenship in my own words.
I can give examples of how people contribute to their communities.
I can identify at least 2 ways I can be civically engaged.
lessons.prior_learning_engagement: Ask: "What is a community? How do people help their community?" Discuss: What does it mean to be an active member of society?
lessons.teaching_learning_activities: Discussion: What is civic engagement? Examples of civic participation (volunteering, voting, advocacy). Case study: UAE community initiatives (Takatof, Red Crescent). Group activity: Brainstorm ways students can be civically engaged. Read textbook pp. 4-9. Key terms: civic engagement, active citizenship, volunteering, advocacy, community service, social responsibility, loyalty.
lessons.assessment_closure: Exit Ticket: Write one way you can be civically engaged in your school or community. Thumbs Up/Thumbs Down: Identify scenarios as civic engagement or not.
lessons.resources_materials:
MSC Grade 9 Student Book pp. 4-9
Case study cards
Plickers / MS Forms
Whiteboard
lessons.homework_extension: Ask a family member about a time they helped the community. Write a short summary.

lessons.learning_objective: SWBAT deepen their understanding of civic engagement by analyzing different forms of civic participation and evaluating the impact of civic action on communities.
lessons.success_criteria:
I can explain the difference between volunteering, advocacy, and community service.
I can analyze the impact of a civic engagement example.
I can propose a solution to a community issue using civic engagement principles.
lessons.prior_learning_engagement: Review: Name 3 types of civic engagement from last week. Ask: "What motivates people to get involved in their community?"
lessons.teaching_learning_activities: Case studies analysis: 3 different civic engagement scenarios (volunteering, advocacy, community organizing). Impact evaluation: How does civic engagement change communities? Role-play: Students take on the role of community advocates and propose solutions to a local issue. Read textbook pp. 4-9 (continued).
lessons.assessment_closure: Action Plan: Students create a simple civic engagement plan for a school or community issue. Peer feedback on plans.
lessons.resources_materials:
MSC Grade 9 Student Book pp. 4-9
Case study handouts
Action plan template
MS Teams / Zoom
lessons.homework_extension: Implement one small civic action this week (help a neighbor, clean up, tutor someone). Write a reflection.

lessons.learning_objective: SWBAT define what it means to be a good citizen, identify qualities such as respect, responsibility, and resourcefulness, and describe the civic virtues that strengthen communities.
lessons.success_criteria:
I can identify and describe at least 3 qualities of a good citizen.
I can explain the difference between following laws and being a good citizen.
I can analyze scenarios and determine if someone is demonstrating good citizenship.
lessons.prior_learning_engagement: Ask: "What makes someone a good citizen? Name 3 qualities." Discuss: Is being a good citizen the same as following the law?
lessons.teaching_learning_activities: Qualities of a good citizen: respectful, responsible, resourceful, honest, compassionate. Civic virtues: tolerance, cooperation, integrity. Scenario analysis: Is this person a good citizen? Why or why not? Read textbook pp. 10-15. Key terms: good citizen, civic virtue, respect, responsibility, resourcefulness, tolerance, integrity, cooperation.
lessons.assessment_closure: Scenario Response: Given 3 scenarios, students identify which civic virtue is demonstrated and explain why. Self-assessment: Which civic virtue do I practice most? Which do I need to improve?
lessons.resources_materials:
MSC Grade 9 Student Book pp. 10-15
Scenario cards
Civic virtue chart
Plickers / MS Forms
lessons.homework_extension: Practice one civic virtue this week. Keep a journal of your actions and reflect on the impact.

lessons.lesson_title: The Good Citizens in My Country
lessons.learning_objective: SWBAT identify the key duties and responsibilities of a good citizen in the UAE, and understand how these duties operate at the family, community, and national levels.
lessons.success_criteria:
I can list at least 5 responsibilities of a good citizen in the UAE.
I can categorize duties at the family, community, and national levels.
I can explain why good citizenship matters for the UAE's development.
lessons.prior_learning_engagement: Review: What are 3 qualities of a good citizen? Ask: "What are YOUR duties as a resident or citizen of the UAE?"
lessons.teaching_learning_activities: Duties and responsibilities at 3 levels: Family (respecting elders, contributing to household), Community (volunteering, following rules, helping neighbors), Nation (respecting laws, national pride, environmental responsibility). UAE-specific duties: Loyalty, respecting laws, observing customs, contributing to development. Read textbook pp. 16-21. Key terms: duty, responsibility, family, community, nation, loyalty, customs, national pride.
lessons.assessment_closure: Three-Level Chart: Students list 2+ duties at each level (family, community, nation). Reflection writing: "I am a good citizen when I..." (complete in 5+ sentences).
lessons.resources_materials:
MSC Grade 9 Student Book pp. 16-21
Three-level chart template
Reflection writing template
Plickers / MS Forms
lessons.homework_extension: Interview a family member about their civic duties. Write a short report comparing their duties to yours.

lessons.lesson_title: The Good Citizens in My Country (Continued)
lessons.learning_objective: SWBAT apply their understanding of UAE citizenship by analyzing real-world examples of good citizenship and reflecting on their own civic identity and responsibilities.
lessons.success_criteria:
I can identify a real-world example of good citizenship in the UAE.
I can articulate my own civic identity and values.
I can set a personal civic goal and explain why it matters.
lessons.prior_learning_engagement: Review: Name 2 duties at each level (family, community, nation). Ask: "Who is a good citizen that you admire? What do they do?"
lessons.teaching_learning_activities: Real-world examples: UAE community leaders and their contributions. Profile analysis: What makes someone a model citizen? Self-reflection: My civic identity — who am I as a citizen? Group discussion: How can our class practice good citizenship? Action planning: Set a personal civic goal for the term. Read textbook pp. 16-21 (continued).
lessons.assessment_closure: Civic Identity Profile: Students write a personal civic profile (5+ sentences) identifying their values, duties, and a specific civic goal. Peer sharing and feedback.
lessons.resources_materials:
MSC Grade 9 Student Book pp. 16-21
Civic identity profile template
UAE community leader profiles
Goal-setting template
lessons.homework_extension: Work on your civic goal this week. Write a reflection on your progress at the end of the week.

lessons.learning_objective: SWBAT demonstrate mastery of Lessons 1–3 through differentiated consolidation worksheets tailored to their learning level, and identify areas for improvement prior to mid-term exams.
lessons.success_criteria:
I can answer assessment questions accurately at my assigned level.
I can identify my areas of strength and areas needing improvement.
I can explain key concepts from the first half of the term.
lessons.prior_learning_engagement: Quick verbal review: 3 key questions from each lesson. Students self-assess confidence on each topic (1-5 scale).
lessons.teaching_learning_activities:
DIFFERENTIATED WORKSHEET ACTIVITY:

Tier 1 (Advanced): Extended analysis worksheet — evaluate the effectiveness of a civic engagement program, compare civic duties across countries, and write a policy recommendation.

Tier 2 (Core): Standard consolidation worksheet — MCQ, short answer, matching, and 1 analytical paragraph about good citizenship in the UAE.

Tier 3 (Scaffolding): Supported worksheet — visual matching, sentence completion, word bank, and guided short-response questions with sentence frames.

After completion: Whole-class review of common gaps.
lessons.assessment_closure:
Tier 1: Evaluated on depth of analysis and quality of recommendation.
Tier 2: Evaluated on accuracy and completeness of responses.
Tier 3: Evaluated on effort and understanding of basic concepts.

Gap analysis: Teacher identifies class-wide and individual weaknesses for targeted review.
lessons.resources_materials:
3 Tiered Worksheet packs (teacher-prepared)
Self-assessment rubric
Plickers / MS Forms
Review answer keys
lessons.homework_extension: Review any topics you found difficult. Prepare questions for the next class.

lessons.learning_objective: SWBAT describe the competencies needed for effective civic engagement (cooperation, teamwork, leadership, advocacy), and analyze real-world examples of community action.
lessons.success_criteria:
I can list key competencies for civic engagement.
I can analyze a real-world community action example.
I can self-assess my own civic competencies and identify areas for growth.
lessons.prior_learning_engagement: Review: What is civic engagement? What makes a good citizen? Ask: "What skills do you need to make a difference in your community?"
lessons.teaching_learning_activities: Competency exploration: Cooperation, teamwork, leadership, advocacy, initiative. Case study: A community action project — how did they organize? What skills did they use? Self-assessment: Which civic competencies do I have? Which do I need to develop? Team challenge: Small groups plan a mini community action project. Read textbook pp. 22-27. Key terms: competencies, cooperation, teamwork, leadership, advocacy, empowerment, initiative, community action.
lessons.assessment_closure: Competency Self-Assessment: Students rate themselves on 5 civic competencies and set a development goal. Team Challenge: Present mini project plan to class.
lessons.resources_materials:
MSC Grade 9 Student Book pp. 22-27
Community story
Self-assessment form
Team challenge materials
Leadership profile template
lessons.homework_extension: Identify one civic competency you want to develop. Write a plan for how you will practice it this week.

lessons.learning_objective: Mid-Term Exams covering Weeks 1–7 content.
lessons.assessment_closure: Mid-Term Written Assessment: MCQ, short answer, scenario analysis, and extended-response covering civic engagement, good citizenship, UAE civic duties, and civic competencies.

lessons.learning_objective: Mid-Term Exams covering Weeks 1–7 content.
lessons.assessment_closure: Mid-Term Written Assessment (continued).

lessons.lesson_title: Youth Civic Engagement & the United Nations
lessons.learning_objective: SWBAT explain how young people can address global challenges, analyze the UN's position on youth engagement, and evaluate youth-led initiatives for climate action and humanitarian response.
lessons.success_criteria:
I can explain how young people can address global challenges.
I can analyze the UN's position on youth engagement.
I can evaluate a youth-led initiative and explain its impact.
lessons.prior_learning_engagement: Review: What are civic competencies? Ask: "Can young people really make a difference in the world? How?"
lessons.teaching_learning_activities: UN and youth engagement: UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), youth programs. Youth climate action: Greta Thunberg, UAE youth climate initiatives. Humanitarian response: How young people help in disasters. Action planning: Students design a youth civic engagement initiative for their school. Read textbook pp. 28-35. Key terms: United Nations, SDGs, youth engagement, climate action, humanitarian, advocacy, global citizenship, initiative.
lessons.assessment_closure: Youth Initiative Proposal: Students design a civic engagement initiative for the school and present it. Peer evaluation using a rubric.
lessons.resources_materials:
MSC Grade 9 Student Book pp. 28-35
UN SDGs poster
Climate action research links
Initiative evaluation rubric
Action plan template
lessons.homework_extension: Research a youth-led initiative anywhere in the world. Write a short evaluation (5-7 sentences).

lessons.learning_objective: SWBAT review and consolidate all Term 3 content through structured revision activities.
lessons.success_criteria:
I can define civic engagement and good citizenship.
I can list civic duties at family, community, and national levels.
I can describe civic competencies and evaluate youth initiatives.
I can explain the UN SDGs and UAE's commitments.
lessons.prior_learning_engagement: Quick-fire quiz: Questions from each week. Students earn points for correct answers.
lessons.teaching_learning_activities: REVIEW STATIONS (Rotation): Station 1: Civic Engagement — define, give examples, analyze scenarios. Station 2: Good Citizenship — qualities, duties, UAE context. Station 3: Civic Competencies — self-assessment, team challenge review. Station 4: Youth & the UN — SDGs, climate action, global citizenship. Station 5: Key Terms — vocabulary matching and application.
lessons.assessment_closure: Revision Quiz (oral + written): MCQ, short answer, scenario analysis, matching, and 1 extended-response question covering all Term 3 topics.
lessons.resources_materials:
All textbook pages used this term
Review station materials
MS Forms / Google Forms
Plickers / Kahoot!
lessons.homework_extension: Review all key terms and concepts. Practice with a family member. Prepare for Final Exams.

lessons.learning_objective: End-of-Term Final Exams covering all Term 3 teaching content.
lessons.assessment_closure: Final Written Assessment: MCQ, short answer, scenario analysis, matching, and extended-response questions covering all Term 3 teaching content (Civic Engagement, Good Citizenship, UAE Civic Duties, Civic Competencies, Youth & the UN).

lessons.learning_objective: End-of-Term Final Exams covering all Term 3 teaching content.
lessons.assessment_closure: Final Written Assessment (continued).

Standards_Reference: Moral, Social & Cultural Studies - MoE Standards Reference | Grades 6-9 (Cycle 2 & 3)
Explains causes of significant historical events; applies chronology, causality, and conflict to identify patterns of historical change.
Identifies national figures who participated in establishing the Union; describes the role played by leaders in nation development.
Analyzes strengths and weaknesses of various kinds of governance systems; compares rights and responsibilities under different systems.
Compares rights and responsibilities under different political systems; defines human rights.
Explains the functions and responsibilities of government leaders and public servants.
Describes how laws are created; explains the differences between civil and criminal law.
Identifies and examines long-term changes and recurring patterns in world history.
Identifies and describes selected countries in terms of their features and challenges.
Explains how technology impacts the environment at different times; analyzes economic development factors.
Uses primary and secondary sources to research about a historical event.
Analyzes geographical characteristics of a place (physical and human); describes origins and expansion of empires.
Gathers and analyzes geographical data; uses maps and digital tools for spatial analysis.
Demonstrate understanding of civic duties and responsibilities at family, community, and national levels.
Explains the responsibilities and duties for all individuals (citizens and non-citizens) in society.
Demonstrate willingness to develop and apply competencies necessary for effective civic engagement.
Identifies opportunities for community action; demonstrates ability to organize and advocate for change.
